claimText: CITES is a legally binding agreement on its parties but does not take the place of national laws.
evidenceExcerpt: Legally binding on Parties, but does not take the place of national laws.
examUse: Statement-based question evaluating the legal enforceability and sovereignty implications of CITES.
distractorHooks: CITES is a voluntary guideline and not legally binding
CITES supersedes and replaces the national wildlife laws of member countries

claimText: India is a party to CITES, having ratified the convention in 1976.
examUse: Statement-based question regarding India's historical participation in international environmental conventions.
distractorHooks: India is an observer state but not a ratified party to CITES
India ratified CITES after the Wildlife Protection Act amendment in the 2000s

claimText: CITES categorizes species into three Appendices based on the degree of protection needed, with Appendix I specifically covering species threatened with extinction.
evidenceExcerpt: CITES Three Appendices: Based on degree of protection needed. Appendix I covers species threatened with extinction; Appendix II covers species needing trade control; Appendix III covers species protected in at least one country asking for assistance.
examUse: Correct-pair or statement-based question testing the criteria for species classification under CITES Appendices.
distractorHooks: Appendix III covers species threatened with extinction
CITES categorizes species into Red, Orange, and Green lists

claimText: The MIKE Programme, CITES Tree Species Programme (CTSP), and the International Consortium on Combating Wildlife Crime (ICCWC) are key initiatives associated with CITES.
evidenceExcerpt: Key Initiatives of CITES: MIKE Programme to monitor illegal killing of elephants; CITES Tree Species Programme (CTSP); International Consortium on Combating Wildlife Crime (ICCWC), 2010.
examUse: Correct-pair or statement-based question matching global environmental initiatives to their parent organizations.
distractorHooks: The MIKE Programme is an initiative of the World Wildlife Fund (WWF)
ICCWC is exclusively managed by the UN Environment Programme (UNEP)

claimText: The World Wildlife Trade Report is published by CITES.
evidenceExcerpt: CITES publishes the World Wildlife Trade Report.
examUse: Correct-pair question matching international reports to their publishing bodies.
distractorHooks: Published by the International Union for Conservation of Nature (IUCN)
